title: Library

jokes.text: President Stevenson encounter at Hughes library — Selina and Gary's panicked 'Oh my God' chorus upon seeing another former president
jokes.setup: Selina is snooping around Hughes' presidential library before the ceremony
jokes.punchline: Gary erupts into a frantic 'Oh my God, oh my God, oh my God, oh my God!' — pure performative anxiety from the world's most high-strung bag man
jokes.explanation: Gary's panic spiral is perfectly on-brand — his entire identity is catastrophizing on Selina's behalf. The physical comedy of two grown adults going 'oh my God' repeatedly plays well as an opener.

jokes.text: Can you even have a library? Seems like it'd be more like a bookmobile.
jokes.setup: President Stevenson is being gracious/condescending to Selina at the Hughes library dedication
jokes.punchline: Selina fires back that Stevenson's presidency was so minor it would only merit a bookmobile, not a full library
jokes.explanation: Sharp, compact, and perfectly presidential — the 'bookmobile' specificity is exactly the kind of precision insult Iannucci specializes in. The image is instantly vivid and humiliating. It earns its high score: only Selina, defending her own presidential legacy while being patronized, would reach for this exact metaphor. Near-flawless economy.

jokes.text: If only the American people could've known you for your sense of humor instead of your bizarre indifference to apartheid.
jokes.setup: Stevenson calls Selina funny with faint praise; Selina seems to accept the compliment
jokes.punchline: She pivots mid-compliment to condemn his apartheid indifference — the setup is a fake-out gracious acceptance
jokes.explanation: This is elite Veep writing. The structure is a classic misdirect: the first clause sounds like a gracious return compliment, then 'instead of your bizarre indifference to apartheid' lands like a grenade. 'Bizarre indifference' is doing heavy lifting — it's not outrage, it's almost clinical disdain, which makes it funnier. High quotability, high craft.

jokes.text: Gary produces a random purse claiming it's a picture of Selina he was tasked to find
jokes.setup: Selina had apparently told Gary to find all pictures of her; he appears mid-conversation brandishing something
jokes.punchline: 'Is that your purse?' — Gary has found an actual handbag instead of a photo, completely earnest
jokes.explanation: Quick background gag on Gary's oblivious loyalty — he went literal on an ambiguous command. Functional character comedy, not a standout.

jokes.text: How they 'Jewed him down,' as Mother used to say when she wasn't busy telling me he only picked me as a running mate so that he could look taller.
jokes.setup: Selina complaining about Hughes stealing opportunities; she references her mother's anti-Semitic saying almost parenthetically
jokes.punchline: The anti-Semitic casual aside is immediately topped by the revelation that her own mother told her she was picked just to make someone look taller — maternal cruelty as a punchline
jokes.explanation: Selina's casual cruelty and her horrific upbringing combine here into a remarkable double-beat joke. The anti-Semitic phrase is deployed as casual period dialogue (her mother), then immediately dethroned by an even more personally devastating revelation. The escalation within a single sentence is sophisticated. High character integration — only Selina's specific biography makes this land.

jokes.text: Oh, my God. Supermodels are your height. / It doesn't matter. She's dead. Who cares?
jokes.setup: Gary tries to comfort Selina about her mother's cruel height comment
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses Gary's consolation with brutal pragmatism: the woman is dead, irrelevant
jokes.explanation: 'She's dead. Who cares?' is perfectly economical Selina — she processes grief and cruelty at the speed of a schedule change. The Gary compliment ('supermodels are your height') being waved away is a perfect beat. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Unlike small-town America, Andrew fucks me in a way I really enjoy.
jokes.setup: Someone worries about how Selina's ex-husband moving back in will look to small-town America
jokes.punchline: Selina bypasses all political calculation with raw personal satisfaction — the political and the sexual collide
jokes.explanation: Precision Veep: the joke works on the contrast between the political frame (optics, small-town America) and Selina's completely un-political answer. 'In a way I really enjoy' is the key phrase — the specificity and satisfaction in it make it funnier than if she'd just said 'the sex is good.' High character integration: Selina's vanity and her complicated relationship with Andrew are both on display.

jokes.text: No Mrs. Hughes. She must be hanging from the rafters somewhere.
jokes.setup: Selina is being photographed with first ladies but notes an absence
jokes.punchline: Casually implies Mrs. Hughes has hanged herself — delivered as social observation
jokes.explanation: Dark but competent — Selina's casual cruelty toward other political wives is well-established. The phrasing 'somewhere' adds a breezy dismissiveness. Solid but not exceptional.

jokes.text: Former fuckers. This is as impromptu as a colonoscopy except with quadruple the assholes.
jokes.setup: Selina learns that former presidents are doing an impromptu photo op without her, leaving her out
jokes.punchline: Double pun: 'assholes' refers both to the colonoscopy metaphor and to the former presidents themselves
jokes.explanation: One of the tightest constructions in the episode. Two lines, two punches: 'Former fuckers' lands first as a pithy label, then the colonoscopy metaphor builds to 'quadruple the assholes' — the double meaning (medical/insult) clicks perfectly. High quotability; this is the kind of line that gets clipped and shared. Selina's specific bitterness about being excluded makes it character-specific.

jokes.text: Richard, you slow down. It'll look like I'm not running. Gary, you go fast. [beat] Oh, no, that's too fast.
jokes.setup: Selina needs to walk away quickly without looking like she's fleeing the former presidents' photo op
jokes.punchline: She micromanages her entourage's pace in real time — Gary is dispatched too fast, then corrected immediately
jokes.explanation: Classic Selina micro-managing physical comedy. The correction beat ('oh, no, that's too fast') lands because of its immediacy. Strong character integration — this is quintessential Selina behavior.

jokes.text: I want a goddamn 'look at the size of my dick,' 'bring history to life' presidential library.
jokes.setup: Selina declares she wants a presidential library; Gary naively suggests giving up the gift wrapping room
jokes.punchline: Selina's vision for her library is expressed entirely in phallic terms — she wants a monument to her ego
jokes.explanation: The juxtaposition of 'look at the size of my dick' with the anodyne 'bring history to life' museum copy is great structural comedy. Selina's ego expressed in explicitly masculine terms is a running theme of the show — her need to dominate in a male-defined arena. Good character integration.

jokes.text: Because you served less than one year? / No. Because nobody gives me any respect.
jokes.setup: Selina asks why she's the only living president without a library
jokes.punchline: Gary offers the obvious factual answer (serving less than a year); Selina ignores it and goes with her persecution narrative
jokes.explanation: A textbook Veep structure: the correct answer is offered, immediately rejected in favor of Selina's self-serving delusion. Gary's timing as inadvertent truth-teller is perfect. High character integration for both.

jokes.text: I was a two-term senator. I was a congresswoman. A mother. / No! I was the first woman vice president.
jokes.setup: Selina lists her accomplishments to justify the library
jokes.punchline: Gary offers 'a mother' as a credential; Selina rejects it with a disgusted 'No!'
jokes.explanation: The 'No!' to motherhood is a perfect encapsulation of Selina's relationship with Catherine. It's funny because it's a credentials list and she violently rejects the most human item. Strong character comedy.

jokes.text: Never forget. / Oh, sorry, that's the Holocaust.
jokes.setup: Selina proclaims 'America cannot forget' her accomplishments; Gary enthusiastically affirms with 'never forget'
jokes.punchline: Gary realizes he's used Holocaust memorial language — 'Oh, sorry, that's the Holocaust. Totally forgot.'
jokes.explanation: The 'Totally forgot' kicker is what elevates this from a decent dark joke to a great one — the self-awareness that forgetting the Holocaust is itself bad, delivered with Gary's cheerful incompetence. Tight structure, very quotable.

jokes.text: I need a monument to Selina Meyer. An institution. / Selina Meyer belongs in an institution.
jokes.setup: Selina declares she needs 'an institution' — meaning a prestigious institution bearing her name
jokes.punchline: Gary (or a staffer) immediately re-interprets 'institution' as a mental institution — which also fits
jokes.explanation: A clean double-meaning joke. 'Belongs in an institution' is a familiar phrasing but the setup earns it because it arises naturally from Selina's own word choice. Solid wordplay with good structural timing.

jokes.text: It was a spa.
jokes.setup: The institution joke lands; someone offhandedly clarifies what the proposed institution actually was
jokes.punchline: 'It was a spa' — implying the 'institution' Selina was associated with was literally a wellness retreat, not a prestigious one
jokes.explanation: The brevity is the joke — three words that recontextualize the 'institution' entirely. Good economy but depends heavily on audience inferring the setup. Slightly confusing without full context.

jokes.text: I wanna talk to every Tom, Dick, and Gehry out there.
jokes.setup: Selina wants to hire an architect for her library and lists requirements
jokes.punchline: 'Tom, Dick, and Gehry' — punning on the idiom 'Tom, Dick, and Harry' by substituting Frank Gehry, the celebrity architect
jokes.explanation: A genuinely excellent pun — the Gehry substitution works perfectly because it's contextually specific (architects, presidential libraries) and the rhythm of 'Tom, Dick, and Harry' makes 'Gehry' land with a satisfying click. This is the kind of wordplay that requires a sharp comedy room. Near-perfect economy — four words do the work.

jokes.text: Kennedy Library is a reference point 'cause, you know, he was also a part-termer.
jokes.setup: Selina compares herself to JFK for library design inspiration
jokes.punchline: The comparison to Kennedy is made purely on the basis of 'part-termer' — reducing one of the most romanticized presidencies to a technicality she shares
jokes.explanation: Nice Selina self-delusion — she finds the only possible equivalence with JFK (both cut short) and deploys it with full confidence. Solid character joke.

jokes.text: That sweaty pederast has ruined more kids than the Common Core.
jokes.setup: Furlong disparages someone (presumably a colleague) in conversation with Will
jokes.punchline: Comparing pederasty to the Common Core on a scale of child-ruining — the educational policy equivalence is the twist
jokes.explanation: Classic Furlong — goes to the most extreme place first (pedophilia) and then pivots to policy (Common Core) in a way that implicates both as child-destroying forces. The juxtaposition is sharp and the Common Core reference dates it perfectly. High originality for the specific comparison.

jokes.text: I'm fortunate to be odious to all colors of the sexual spectrum.
jokes.setup: Furlong claims Will's grotesqueness protects him from a predator
jokes.punchline: Will agrees with complete serenity — he considers being universally repulsive a blessing across all sexual orientations
jokes.explanation: 'All colors of the sexual spectrum' is a masterpiece of Will's specific voice — formal, pseudo-academic, and deeply resigned. The joke works because Will's deadpan acceptance of being universally repulsive is more dignified than Furlong's contempt. Very high quotability; 'all colors of the sexual spectrum' is a standalone phrase. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: So, what is it? Speak, Professor X-Gayvier.
jokes.setup: Furlong wants Will to get to the point
jokes.punchline: 'Professor X-Gayvier' — merging Professor Xavier (X-Men) with a gay slur, producing an absurd superhero portmanteau
jokes.explanation: Furlong's nickname-generating machine is one of Veep's great recurring features. 'X-Gayvier' is inventive if slightly reliant on shock value. The X-Men reference adds a pop-culture layer. Solid Furlong character comedy.

jokes.text: Yeah, let me save you the peanut log you're about to squeeze out of your face anus here... no.
jokes.setup: Jonah/Will is about to make his case for a seat on Ways and Means
jokes.punchline: Furlong preemptively cuts him off with an extended metaphor equating his argument to defecating from his face — 'face anus' and 'peanut log' are the key inventions
jokes.explanation: This is Furlong operating at peak creative cruelty. 'Face anus' is genuinely inventive — the mouth-as-anus metaphor extended into 'peanut log' is Iannucci's insult comedy at its most baroque. The punchline is the dismissive 'no' after all that elaborate setup. High quotability, high originality, maximum character integration for Furlong.

jokes.text: You remain in detention on the Ethics Committee with the rest of the special ed Breakfast Club.
jokes.setup: Furlong denying Jonah/Will's request for a better committee assignment
jokes.punchline: The Ethics Committee is rendered as detention for special ed students; the 'Breakfast Club' reference adds a specific movie-callback dimension
jokes.explanation: Strong Furlong — the Ethics Committee as a powerless dumping ground for congressional misfits is a real political truth rendered as a John Hughes reference. The 'special ed Breakfast Club' is specific enough to be original while landing broadly.

jokes.text: Dismissed, GI Slow.
jokes.setup: Furlong ends the meeting with Jonah's representative
jokes.punchline: 'GI Slow' — a riff on GI Joe that insults the subject's intelligence/speed
jokes.explanation: Efficient Furlong nickname — economy is near-perfect (two words, maximum contempt). The 'GI Slow' pun is not as inventive as his best work but lands cleanly.

jokes.text: I'm free Saturday night. / I am shocked.
jokes.setup: Furlong mentioned his dinner party; Will volunteers his availability
jokes.punchline: Furlong's 'I am shocked' — the most minimal possible sarcasm, perfectly timed
jokes.explanation: The faux-formal 'I am shocked' is funnier than a colloquial 'shocking' would be. Furlong's register of mock astonishment is well-executed. Economy is near-perfect but the concept isn't novel.

jokes.text: Good luck getting your precious back from those mean hobbits, Sméagol.
jokes.setup: Final dismissal of Will before he leaves
jokes.punchline: Comparing Will to Sméagol (the hunched, desperate, obsessive Lord of the Rings character) — implying Will has some 'precious' thing being withheld by people more powerful than him
jokes.explanation: Furlong's pop-culture insult nickname generator — the Sméagol comparison works because it captures Will's desperate, grasping quality. 'Those mean hobbits' is a nice extension. Furlong's string of insults in this scene collectively are a highlight.

jokes.text: I, uh... I'm so sorry, but, you know, a job's a job. / That's a false equivalence, but I appreciate the sentiment.
jokes.setup: Will apologizes to Kent for working under someone like Furlong
jokes.punchline: Kent corrects 'a job's a job' as a logical fallacy — 'that's a false equivalence' — with academic precision and then, touchingly, thanks him
jokes.explanation: Perfect Kent: even in a moment of human connection, he cannot let a logical fallacy pass without correction. The warmth behind 'I appreciate the sentiment' makes it simultaneously sad and funny. Strong character integration — this joke is impossible without Kent's specific voice.

jokes.text: Ma'am, could you please stop dancing? 'Cause I'm just... I'm framing out your dress right now. / You know, you're using an awful lot of paint for down there. Maybe use it for up here and focus on this.
jokes.setup: Selina is dancing and disrupting the portrait painter, who asks her to stop
jokes.punchline: Selina accuses the painter of focusing too much on her lower half, then demands she paint more of her face — the insecurity is played as comic vanity
jokes.explanation: Selina's vanity expressed through portrait direction is solid character comedy. The body image insecurity being weaponized against the painter is very Selina. Not exceptional but functional.

jokes.text: Well, I guess AIDS had a good run.
jokes.setup: A staffer reports that people are more interested in Selina's library than in adult literacy or AIDS
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses the AIDS cause with 'I guess AIDS had a good run' — treating a global epidemic as though it were a TV show getting cancelled
jokes.explanation: This is the best joke in the episode and arguably one of Veep's finest. The phrase 'had a good run' is so perfectly borrowed from the TV-cancellation lexicon — it reframes the AIDS crisis as an entertainment product that ran its course. Six words, delivered deadpan, reveal an abyss of moral vacancy that is both horrifying and hilarious. Extraordinary economy. Maximum character integration — Selina's casual abandonment of causes she nominally championed is the entire show in microcosm. This is what a 9 looks like.

jokes.text: Pakistani industrialist friend who's interested in donating $20 million if you get his cousin off the no-fly list. / As long as he promises not to blow up my library, I mean, I don't really give a shit.
jokes.setup: A staffer proposes a foreign donation contingent on removing someone from the no-fly list
jokes.punchline: Selina's only real concern is whether he'll blow up her library — she's explicitly not concerned about national security, only her legacy
jokes.explanation: Selina's priorities expressed with crystal clarity — her library outranks national security. The 'I don't really give a shit' is the perfect closer. Strong character integration; borderline dark in a satisfying way.

jokes.text: I've been standing here the entire time, ma'am. Just trying to keep still in case I'm in the painting.
jokes.setup: Selina calls for Richard as if he's absent
jokes.punchline: Richard has been in the room the whole time, motionless, hoping to be included in the portrait
jokes.explanation: Richard Splett is one of Veep's great comedic creations — his earnest, literal-minded optimism is on full display. 'Just trying to keep still in case I'm in the painting' is exactly the kind of thought only Richard would have. High character integration.

jokes.text: I really wanna have a reflecting pool someplace for people to come and sit and reflect on what this cocksuck of a country did to me.
jokes.setup: Selina is planning the library and mentions wanting a reflecting pool
jokes.punchline: The reflecting pool — a solemn, Lincoln Memorial-type institution — is repurposed as a monument to Selina's grievances against America
jokes.explanation: Selina subverts the most solemn symbol of national reflection (the Lincoln Memorial reflecting pool) and turns it into a monument to personal grievance. 'What this cocksuck of a country did to me' is a perfect encapsulation of her entire worldview — narcissism married to genuine victimhood. Extremely high quotability.

jokes.text: My pen's just out of ink. I'm gonna scratch it into the paper and then kind of go back over and trace over and see what I wrote before, like in a suspense movie.
jokes.setup: Richard needs to take notes but his pen is out of ink
jokes.punchline: His solution — scratch the paper and trace indentations like a movie forensics scene — is delivered with complete earnestness as though it's a normal business solution
jokes.explanation: Richard's problem-solving is always gold — his cheerful improvisation with complete confidence in a ridiculous method is classic Splett. 'Like in a suspense movie' elevates it.

jokes.text: They said the Sherman Tanz pardon was unsavory and not in line with the values of Yale. Then they asked for a donation to their annual fund.
jokes.setup: Yale rejects Selina on ethical grounds
jokes.punchline: Then immediately asks for money — the hypocrisy of elite institutions refusing someone on moral grounds while soliciting donations is the joke
jokes.explanation: The punchline is a sharp piece of institutional satire — Yale's moral rejection immediately followed by a funding ask is a real thing elite schools do. Solid observational comedy.

jokes.text: Just like a Smith girl, open to exploring. / Lesbians would really know how to run a library.
jokes.setup: Smith College (a historically women's college) says they're 'open to exploring' hosting the library
jokes.punchline: Selina makes a lesbian joke about Smith women — again revealing her casual prejudices while ostensibly championing women
jokes.explanation: The Smith/lesbian joke is a bit familiar but 'Lesbians would really know how to run a library' is a funny non-sequitur. The Selina hypocrisy angle adds depth. Competent.

jokes.text: Fuck Yale University. / Fuck Yale. / I'm not really comfortable with that kind of language. / Fuck Yale University.
jokes.setup: The group decides to pivot from Yale to Smith; Selina declares 'Fuck Yale'
jokes.punchline: The chant builds, someone expresses discomfort, Selina repeats it louder — the collective profanity becomes a bonding ritual
jokes.explanation: The escalation beat where one person's discomfort only makes Selina louder is classic Veep group dynamics. The second 'Fuck Yale University' after the objection is the kicker.

jokes.text: Mike can't find his daughter Ellen — 'I'm here, Mike! I'm going to hide again!' / How the fuck long was that kid here for? / I think just since today, but I can't be sure.
jokes.setup: A child has apparently been loose in Selina's office for an indeterminate amount of time during a working meeting
jokes.punchline: Gary's 'I think just since today, but I can't be sure' — the uncertainty about when a child entered the room is perfectly absurd
jokes.explanation: Gary's 'I can't be sure' is the gem — the idea that a child could have been in this room long enough that nobody can pinpoint her arrival is perfectly absurd. The enthusiastic 'I'm going to hide again!' from Ellen adds to the chaos.

jokes.text: I'm sure that there are some bored housewives out there that would enjoy ogling your Walter Cronkite, but to me, it looks like local morning news from, I don't know, Delaware.
jokes.setup: Jane is reviewing Danny's piece on the Polar Bear Club — it's clearly amateur
jokes.punchline: 'Walter Cronkite' as a euphemism for Danny's sex appeal, then the Delaware insult — local Delaware news as shorthand for bottom-tier television
jokes.explanation: 'Walter Cronkite' as Dan/Danny's appeal is smart — implies his appeal is to an older demographic. Delaware as the ultimate insult for 'nowhere' TV is specific and funny. Jane's cutting newsroom persona is well-served.

jokes.text: No, it's so Delaware.
jokes.setup: Jane says the piece looks like Delaware local news
jokes.punchline: Someone immediately agrees — 'so Delaware' as a shorthand for the worst possible broadcast production value
jokes.explanation: A quick callback that reinforces the Delaware insult. Brief but effective. The agreement makes the 'Delaware' become a self-contained modifier.

jokes.text: 'Not the best fit' the piece or 'not the best fit' you and me? / Mostly the piece.
jokes.setup: Jane diplomatically says the piece isn't 'the best fit'; Danny awkwardly extends the phrase to their relationship
jokes.punchline: 'Mostly the piece' — Jane admits the relationship is also somewhat not the best fit, but the piece is the primary problem
jokes.explanation: 'Mostly the piece' is a perfect cringe-comedy delivery — the qualifier 'mostly' does all the work. Jane's refusal to fully commit either way is very in-character for a news-world climber. Well-constructed.

jokes.text: I am a balling bachelor sexual congressman, and I will be passing bills by day and smashing gash by night.
jokes.setup: Jonah is advised that being a bachelor is politically problematic
jokes.punchline: Jonah's self-description as a 'balling bachelor sexual congressman' with a self-styled slogan about 'passing bills / smashing gash' is his delusional grandiosity at full pitch
jokes.explanation: 'Balling bachelor sexual congressman' is a Jonah-specific construction — the accumulation of adjectives, each one trying harder than the last, is perfect Jonah. 'Passing bills by day and smashing gash by night' has the rhythm of a campaign slogan, which makes it funnier. High character integration; this could only come from Jonah Ryan.

jokes.text: Congressman, being a bachelor in this town means you're gayer than a TV evangelist.
jokes.setup: Jonah insists on his bachelor lifestyle; Kent provides the political reality
jokes.punchline: 'Gayer than a TV evangelist' — the inversion of the TV evangelist as secretly gay (a real cultural phenomenon) delivered with Kent's signature statistical detachment
jokes.explanation: Kent's political assessment delivered as empirical fact — 'gayer than a TV evangelist' is the right level of specific cultural reference that lands as both an insult and a genuine political observation. The TV evangelist comparison is original and well-observed. High quotability.

jokes.text: God damn it! I hate homophobia.
jokes.setup: Kent's 'gayer than a TV evangelist' remark implies being gay is a political problem
jokes.punchline: Jonah objects to homophobia — while clearly having only self-interested reasons for the objection (he doesn't want to be perceived as gay)
jokes.explanation: Jonah performing progressive values for entirely selfish reasons is on-brand. 'God damn it! I hate homophobia.' has the rhythm of someone who just learned the word and is deploying it.

jokes.text: Statistically speaking, married politicians have more sex with more single women than single politicians.
jokes.setup: Jonah is resistant to marriage; Kent explains why he should want it
jokes.punchline: Kent's argument for marriage is purely statistical — and the statistic is that married politicians cheat more, therefore get more sex
jokes.explanation: A genuinely brilliant Kent joke — the deliverable is appalling (cheat more, therefore more sex) but the framing is impeccably statistical and logical. The joke works because Kent is persuasively endorsing infidelity as a productivity metric. High character integration — only Kent would present this as a rational argument. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: Fine. Find me Mrs. Ryan. / Okay. / Not my mom.
jokes.setup: Jonah capitulates and asks Kent to find him a wife
jokes.punchline: 'Not my mom' — the qualifier suggests that without the caveat, his mom might have been a viable candidate, revealing something genuinely disturbing about Jonah's relationships
jokes.explanation: 'Not my mom' is three words that imply a universe of dysfunction. The economy is near-perfect. It's funny because the clarification only makes sense if it needed clarifying — implying Jonah has a notably close or ambiguous relationship with his mother. Very quotable and punchy.

jokes.text: Watching the Nevada candidate Buddy Calhoun cry on video: 'He's crying. Haha, what a fucking girl!'
jokes.setup: Buddy Calhoun is shown tearfully defending his wife's honor in a political ad.
jokes.punchline: Amy's immediate, gleefully sexist dismissal of a political opponent's tears — 'what a fucking girl' — shows her total detachment from any feminist instinct in a professional context.
jokes.explanation: Works as a character beat for Amy's political ruthlessness — she dismisses the emotion with exactly the language she'd condemn if it were used against her. Not particularly original but fits Amy perfectly.

jokes.text: Oh, what did you envision, Woody, that you and Buzz Lightyear would win this election and Andy would play with you again?
jokes.setup: A campaign manager (Buddy's) expresses disappointment at how the campaign is going
jokes.punchline: Amy compares them to Toy Story characters — implying the manager is a discarded toy hoping to be played with again by the public (Andy)
jokes.explanation: Amy's creative contempt finding the right pop-culture frame is pleasing. The Toy Story metaphor actually works on a thematic level (discarded politicians seeking renewed relevance) which gives it depth beyond simple name-dropping.

jokes.text: All right, hit 'POTUS SUV.' Now select playlist. And voila.
jokes.setup: Dan wants to show something on the dashcam footage system from the presidential vehicle
jokes.punchline: The ease with which he navigates what should be secure presidential vehicle systems to replay video of a crying politician — 'voila' as the punctuation
jokes.explanation: The joke is more about what they're doing (accessing presidential vehicle footage casually) than the dialogue itself. Functional but not memorable.

jokes.text: Also, estimates for construction are coming in at $300 million. / What? / The money's out there. We just can't get our hands on it right now.
jokes.setup: Construction estimates come in at $300 million for the library
jokes.punchline: The calm reassurance that 'the money's out there' is simultaneously meaningless and stated with full confidence
jokes.explanation: The money is 'out there but we can't access it' is standard Veep financial dysfunction comedy. Functional.

jokes.text: Can we reallocate some of the money from the Meyer Fund to the library? / Absolutely, yes. / That's actually a felony. / So, we just tell them that we have it all... And then we just shuffle papers around until it looks like we actually do. / That's also a felony.
jokes.setup: Selina asks about reallocating charitable funds to her vanity library project
jokes.punchline: Two consecutive felonies — Gary enthusiastically agrees to the first, a lawyer-type flatly calls it a felony; Selina's workaround is also a felony
jokes.explanation: The escalation structure — Gary enthusiastic agreement, lawyer objection, Selina workaround, same objection — is tight and well-paced. 'That's also a felony' as a repeated beat earns its second use.

jokes.text: In order to find an ideal match, the restaurant, table, food, and time will be the same for every date to establish a control. The girl will be different.
jokes.setup: Kent is setting up a dating system for Jonah
jokes.punchline: 'The girl will be different' — announced as a variable in an experiment, not a human relationship
jokes.explanation: Kent reducing romance to a controlled scientific experiment and casually noting 'the girl will be different' as though she's the only variable is one of his finest moments. The structure — elaborate setup of constants, then the stark 'girl will be different' — is impeccably constructed. Near-maximum character integration: this joke is 100% Kent Davidson.

jokes.text: Jesus, Kent. He's just gotta go to a Laundromat, meet a girl, take her to a movie, a little chit-chat, couple-three drinks, standard hayjay in the cab home, and a year later, he's a father.
jokes.setup: Kent's overly scientific dating approach is challenged by Dan
jokes.punchline: 'Standard hayjay in the cab home' — the cheerful normalization of a cab handjob as a standard relationship milestone, plus 'couple-three drinks' as casual Midwest phrasing
jokes.explanation: 'Standard hayjay' is a funny euphemism, and the nonchalant roadmap from laundromat to fatherhood compresses romance into assembly instructions. 'Couple-three drinks' has a genuine regional flavor.

jokes.text: One time, I tried hitting on a girl at a Laundromat. For somebody with broken English, she was real uppity.
jokes.setup: Dan endorses the Laundromat approach from personal experience
jokes.punchline: The experience is immediately characterized by xenophobia and entitlement — 'for somebody with broken English, she was real uppity' reveals Dan's worldview
jokes.explanation: Dan Egan's self-incrimination is always enjoyable — the casual bigotry and entitlement in 'for somebody with broken English, she was real uppity' tells you everything about him. The joke is the revelation of character, not the observation itself. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Every potential mate has been preselected from the online dating pool of DC women ages 25 to 30 with a strong interest in marriage to you in particular.
jokes.setup: Kent explains his dating database for Jonah
jokes.punchline: The filter 'strong interest in marriage to you in particular' — as if women have specified Jonah Ryan as their desired spouse on a dating profile
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of 'strong interest in marriage to you in particular' as a filter criterion is very funny — who would select for that? Kent's confidence in the methodology is the joke.

jokes.text: Son of a bitch. I'm gonna go broke paying for all those dates. / Unlikely. There's only four.
jokes.setup: Jonah assumes the dating pool is large
jokes.punchline: Kent flatly corrects: only four women in the DC area meet the criteria
jokes.explanation: Kent's 'Unlikely. There's only four.' is a clean two-beat — the correction implies Jonah is so unmarriageable that only four women in the capital would consider him. The implication is the joke.

jokes.text: I think that title is a bit of a word salad. Maybe trim that last part about women.
jokes.setup: The library's proposed name includes 'School for Public Policy and Women's Studies'
jokes.punchline: The president of a women's college suggests removing 'women' from the name — the irony of a women's institution suggesting this for PR reasons
jokes.explanation: The irony of a women's college president urging the removal of 'women' from the name is a sharp satirical point about institutional self-censorship. Well-observed.

jokes.text: And of course you would be offered a full tenured professorship. / Oh, now, would that mean I would have... Teach? Oh, no. God, no. Because my schedule... / Your schedule... / is just constantly... / in flux. / fucked.
jokes.setup: Smith offers Selina a professorship
jokes.punchline: Selina's horror at the idea of actually teaching, then the synchronized completion of her sentence where the staffer says 'fucked' and she says 'in flux'
jokes.explanation: The simultaneous 'in flux' / 'fucked' is a great structural beat — both say the same thing, one sanitized, one honest. The gap between Selina's public decorum and her actual view ('fucked') is Veep in miniature. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: Chardonnay on the quad after Julia Child Day? / You know what, I'm strictly a scotch girl and I always have been, so I never really experimented... with Chardonnay.
jokes.setup: The Smith president reminisces about a specific night in college — clearly implying a sexual encounter ('Chardonnay on the quad' and 'experimented')
jokes.punchline: Selina redirects to Chardonnay as a literal drink she never experimented with — 'I never really experimented... with Chardonnay' — the pause and the pivot are the joke
jokes.explanation: The ellipsis before 'with Chardonnay' is where the comedy lives — that pause lets the audience complete the sentence before Selina redirects. The deflection is total and convincing while being obviously false. The structure is near-perfect: setup, expectation, misdirection, confirmation of misdirection ('I don't think I was confused'). Strong Selina character work.

jokes.text: I don't think I was confused. / Good for you.
jokes.setup: Selina has claimed the Smith president has her confused with someone else regarding the college night
jokes.punchline: The president firmly says 'I don't think I was confused' — she knows what she remembers. Selina's 'Good for you' is the perfect non-answer.
jokes.explanation: 'Good for you' is Selina at her most deflective — acknowledging without acknowledging, moving on without accepting. Perfectly economical. The callback to the Chardonnay moment makes it a two-beat comedic construction.

jokes.text: Not that it matters, but as a nephew of a survivor of the Holocaust... Sherman Tanz is particularly sensitive to prison conditions. And he is intimately involved with every aspect of his company. Now, that being said, he had no idea what was going on.
jokes.setup: Students ask about the Sherman Tanz pardon and abuse in his prisons
jokes.punchline: Gary's defense of Tanz is constructed entirely of contradictions: Holocaust sensitivity, intimate involvement, no knowledge — all in the same breath
jokes.explanation: The structure of Gary's non-defense is genuinely well-constructed: every claim undermines the next. 'Intimately involved' followed immediately by 'had no idea what was going on' is the perfect logical contradiction delivered with total earnestness. The Holocaust opener as reputation-laundering is dark and sharp.

jokes.text: He immediately fired the number five man in charge as well as a couple of sixes.
jokes.setup: Gary is explaining that Tanz took corrective action by firing those responsible
jokes.punchline: 'The number five man in charge' — a very specific hierarchical designation that implies Tanz protected everyone more important; 'a couple of sixes' makes it worse
jokes.explanation: This is a great joke. The specificity of 'number five man in charge' (not number one, two, three, or four) is precisely observed corporate accountability theater — scapegoating the powerless while protecting the powerful. 'A couple of sixes' extends it perfectly. High originality, strong structure.

jokes.text: Sadly, it's what I've come to expect from the gatekeepers of this patriarchal... 'phallus quo.'
jokes.setup: Selina is defending herself against the Sherman Tanz questions using feminist framing
jokes.punchline: She mispronounces/mishears 'status quo' as 'phallus quo' — an accidentally perfect portmanteau that she immediately catches with horror
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's peak jokes. 'Phallus quo' is a brilliant accidental portmanteau — it's the perfect word for what she's describing (the patriarchal status quo) delivered in the worst possible moment. The layered irony is extraordinary: she's making a feminist argument and accidentally invents a phallic term for the system she's attacking. The structure (building feminist rhetoric → horrified slip → the slip is actually more accurate than the original) is near-perfect. Selina's immediate mortification adds the reaction beat. Very high craft.

jokes.text: I apologize. / Phallus!
jokes.setup: Selina says 'phallus quo' and immediately tries to apologize
jokes.punchline: A student shouts 'Phallus!' in gleeful reaction — adding insult to injury
jokes.explanation: The student's delighted shout is a great reaction beat — the room turning the disaster into a party. The callback nature makes it land harder. The single-word exclamation is maximum economy.

jokes.text: That's our first woman president, y'all! Yeah, holla, holla, holla.
jokes.setup: Selina has just said 'phallus quo' in front of a room of students
jokes.punchline: A student ironically cheers Selina as 'our first woman president' immediately after her humiliation — the enthusiasm is sincere in its mockery
jokes.explanation: The ironic cheer captures the student's Schadenfreude nicely. 'Y'all' and 'holla, holla, holla' give it an authentic college-student voice. Functional capper to the sequence.

jokes.text: I hope one of them is changing out of that shroud of urine.
jokes.setup: Mike says he has 'exciting ideas' for the memoir prologue
jokes.punchline: Selina responds to Mike's baby-related appearance — presumably covered in some kind of baby fluid — as wearing a 'shroud of urine'
jokes.explanation: 'Shroud of urine' is a beautiful phrase — the religious/funerary connotation ('shroud') contrasted with the pedestrian 'urine' is exactly the Veep register. Selina's disdain rendered in baroque language.

jokes.text: Wear something tight. / Huh? / That's not for them, that's for me. / Oh, you are bad!
jokes.setup: Andrew gives advice about the fundraiser — 'wear something tight'
jokes.punchline: It's for his personal benefit, not strategic — Selina's delighted 'Oh, you are bad!' seals it
jokes.explanation: Gets a rewatch bonus given what's about to happen with Andrew's infidelity — this moment of apparently charming flirtation is revealed to be deeply hollow. In isolation, it's functional romance-comedy. The dramatic irony elevates it retrospectively.

jokes.text: Remember in seventh grade when you ran away? You were crying and crying 'cause you wanted Daddy and I to get back together. / Then you sent me to etiquette camp. / Yes, where you learned to interrupt, evidently.
jokes.setup: Selina invokes a childhood memory of Catherine wanting her parents together
jokes.punchline: Catherine points out Selina's response was etiquette camp; Selina turns this into a criticism of Catherine's interrupting
jokes.explanation: 'Yes, where you learned to interrupt, evidently' — Selina pivoting from maternal negligence to a petty criticism in the same breath is perfect. The speed of the deflection is the joke.

jokes.text: Marjorie and I are having a baby. / What? / This is wonderful, wonderful news. / Wait a minute, what? One of you is pregnant?
jokes.setup: Catherine announces she and Marjorie are having a baby
jokes.punchline: Selina's first reaction is 'wonderful news' (Gary-style performance); her second beat is genuine confusion about the biology
jokes.explanation: Selina's double-take is well-executed — first the automatic 'wonderful news' performance, then the actual processing. Her genuine confusion ('one of you is pregnant?') is realistic and humanizing.

jokes.text: If Catherine's uterus is as loamy as the doctor says, you're gonna be a grand... ma'am.
jokes.setup: Discussion of Catherine and Marjorie having a baby — Selina will be a grandmother
jokes.punchline: 'Grand-ma'am' — Gary's portmanteau of 'grandmother' and Selina's title ('ma'am'), delivered with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: This is an exceptional joke. 'Grand-ma'am' is a perfect Gary invention — it combines his total devotion to Selina's title with a genuine attempt to be helpful about the grandmother news. 'Loamy uterus' as a prelude is also very funny. The pause before 'ma'am' lets the audience realize what's coming. Near-top character integration: only Gary would say this. One of the episode's best.

jokes.text: But I'm only 49.
jokes.setup: Gary announces Selina will be a 'grand-ma'am'
jokes.punchline: Selina's only concern is her age — not emotion, not the miracle of life, just 'but I'm only 49'
jokes.explanation: Five words that perfectly encapsulate Selina's values — vanity above all. Not the baby's wellbeing, not Catherine's happiness. Her age. Strong character integration, perfect economy.

jokes.text: Hey, just so you guys know, you gotta be careful with sperm banks. Did you hear about the one in Georgia? Turns out the guy was actually a mentally ill felon. / Oh, my God. Can you imagine having a baby in Georgia?
jokes.setup: The family discusses sperm donors; Andrew warns about a sperm bank scandal
jokes.punchline: Selina's concern is not about the mentally ill felon's genetics but about the horror of living in Georgia — state snobbery above child welfare
jokes.explanation: Selina's geographic snobbery winning out over actual genetic concerns is perfectly calibrated. Andrew's story sets up a legitimate worry; Selina pivots to the location. The juxtaposition is fast and the prioritization is comic gold.

jokes.text: Why don't you use Gary's sperm? It's just sitting there gathering dust.
jokes.setup: The family needs a trusted sperm donor they know
jokes.punchline: Andrew casually volunteers Gary's sperm — 'just sitting there gathering dust' — implying Gary is celibate and his genetic material is wasted
jokes.explanation: 'Just sitting there gathering dust' is a memorably crass image for Gary's implied celibacy. The casualness with which Andrew volunteers someone else's sperm for his grandchild is very Andrew. Good economy and character specificity.

jokes.text: Do you know how they remove a cancerous testicle? [explanation of the surgical procedure] Oh, and I know what you're thinking. The one that I still have, like, it works just fine. / Of course. / All right, so who's hungry?
jokes.setup: Jonah is on a date; his date launches into an unsolicited medical description of testicular cancer surgery
jokes.punchline: The revelation that she has had a testicle removed, the reassurance about the remaining one's function, then Jonah's 'All right, so who's hungry?' — his date pivoting to dinner as though this were normal conversation
jokes.explanation: A beautifully escalating cringe sequence. The surgical description is already uncomfortable; 'The one that I still have, like, it works just fine' takes it further by revealing it's personal; then 'All right, so who's hungry?' is a perfect pivot that treats the TMI as completely normal. The reverse-cringe (Jonah, of all people, being put off) is funny given his own awfulness. High originality for the date-from-hell scenario.

jokes.text: Dessert, then. I got a sweet tooth. / Okay. / Question... do you have any current pictures of your mother? I'm gonna need both front and rear.
jokes.setup: Jonah's date accepts the dessert restriction; the conversation seems to be recovering
jokes.punchline: She requests current photos of Jonah's mother — 'front and rear' — revealing something deeply concerning about her intentions
jokes.explanation: Superb escalation. After the testicle conversation, this date could not possibly get weirder — and then it does. 'I'm gonna need both front and rear' of Jonah's mother is a perfectly calibrated shock line that the audience can't quite categorize (is she a stalker? A fetishist? Just deeply weird?). The indeterminacy makes it funnier. 'I just gotta run to the bathroom' as Jonah's exit is the perfect button.

jokes.text: Oh, the bathrooms are that way.
jokes.setup: Jonah excuses himself to the bathroom after the mother photo request
jokes.punchline: The date helpfully points out where the bathrooms are — she has no awareness she's been abandoned
jokes.explanation: The oblivious helpfulness is the joke — she's been so strange that she doesn't register his flight as flight. Good button to the date sequence.

jokes.text: David Sloane, hedge fund manager. He's in for a million. 1.5 if we can get his dyslexic daughter into Smith. / Well, maybe get her to write the check. Get 5.1 out of him.
jokes.setup: A donor's contribution is contingent on his daughter getting into Smith
jokes.punchline: Selina suggests getting the dyslexic daughter to write the check — implying the transcription error (5.1 instead of 1.5) would be a profitable mistake
jokes.explanation: This might be the most structurally elegant joke in the episode. The setup is a simple donor brief; the punchline works on two levels — it's both a cruel joke about dyslexia AND a genuinely clever financial scheme (1.5 reversed = 5.1). The joke rewards people who do the math. Selina's willingness to exploit a disability for profit is maximum character integration. Very close to the AIDS joke in quality. High originality.

jokes.text: You have three text messages from Helen Wright. / Is that my phone? / No, my pants didn't vibrate.
jokes.setup: A phone announces text messages from Helen Wright; Andrew plays dumb about whose phone it is
jokes.punchline: 'No, my pants didn't vibrate' — Andrew denying ownership of the messages with an oddly specific physical alibi
jokes.explanation: The 'pants didn't vibrate' alibi is too specific to be innocent — an inadvertent tell. Rewatch bonus: on second viewing, the denial is obviously false. Works as setup for the message reading.

jokes.text: Message number one: 'I can still taste you in my mouth from last night.'
jokes.setup: Selina's assistant's phone reads messages aloud; first message is from Helen Wright
jokes.punchline: The first text is explicitly sexual — 'I can still taste you in my mouth from last night' — revealed through the phone speaker in a roomful of people
jokes.explanation: The first reveal in a devastating sequence. The comedy of the phone reading aloud what should be private is well-established, and the directness of the text makes the silence after it land hard.

jokes.text: That's probably to her boyfriend. I imagine she's texting while riding her bicycle and sent it to me by mistake.
jokes.setup: The first text ('I can still taste you in my mouth') is revealed in the room
jokes.punchline: Andrew's desperate improvised explanation — bicycle, wrong recipient, boyfriend — is absurd in its specificity and implausibility
jokes.explanation: The more specific Andrew's alibi, the more obvious the guilt. 'Riding her bicycle' is a beautiful improvised detail — it's plausible enough to be worth trying and absurd enough to reveal the panic. Strong character work for Andrew.

jokes.text: Second message: 'You came so much. Andrew, we ruined your wife's pretty office chair. I get so hot thinking of the president sitting in our love puddle. Call me after the fundraiser.' / That was a long one. She must be at a light.
jokes.setup: After the first shocking text, a second even more explicit one is read aloud
jokes.punchline: Andrew's response to 'love puddle / office chair' is 'that was a long one, she must be at a light' — still maintaining the cycling alibi, now escalated to absurdity
jokes.explanation: The 'love puddle' text is already a standout phrase; the fact that 'the president' is mentioned makes the humiliation total. But the genius is Andrew's response: he doubles down on the bicycle alibi with 'she must be at a light.' The callback to the bicycle (she had more time to text because she stopped at a traffic light) is structurally brilliant and reveals Andrew's brazen willingness to commit to an insane cover story. Peak cringe-escalation. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: Look, Lee... / Not one fucking word.
jokes.setup: Andrew begins to attempt an explanation to Selina
jokes.punchline: 'Not one fucking word' — Selina cuts him off before he can speak, the fury rendered in perfect economy
jokes.explanation: Economy is near-perfect. The 'Lee' (Andrew's nickname for Selina) adding intimacy to the moment before being shut down is a nice micro-detail. The line works because we've earned the emotional weight through the text sequence.

jokes.text: Gary attacking Andrew — 'I will kill you in your face!'
jokes.setup: Andrew's infidelity has been revealed; Gary, Selina's protector, reacts
jokes.punchline: 'I will kill you in your face!' — Gary's rage is both genuine and grammatically confused; the oddly specific 'in your face' makes the threat absurd
jokes.explanation: 'Kill you in your face' is a wonderfully specific and slightly incoherent threat — Gary's rage overriding his grammar. It's funny because Gary's protective fury toward Selina is the most human thing about him, expressed in the most Gary-ish way possible. High character integration; this is Gary's ultimate form.

jokes.text: So, luckily, the doctors were able to remove the mass without a full mastectomy, so I got to keep my nipple. Whoo-hoo, right? / I'm gonna go ahead and be honest with you. That was the most disgusting story I've ever heard. Now I can't... I can't finish my chicken piccata.
jokes.setup: Selina is working the fundraiser room; a donor has apparently been telling a medical story
jokes.punchline: Selina, in full crisis mode from the Andrew texts, responds to 'I got to keep my nipple / Whoo-hoo!' with brutal candor — she tells the donor it's the most disgusting story she's ever heard and she can't finish her chicken
jokes.explanation: Selina's internal state (the Andrew texts) removing all social filtering is a great comedy device. 'Most disgusting story I've ever heard' at a fundraiser is peak Selina crisis behavior. 'Chicken piccata' as the specific casualty is a nice detail.

jokes.text: Andrew is very hard to get rid of. He's like the herpes virus or an unwanted child. In this case, he gave me both.
jokes.setup: Selina is venting about Andrew to Amy over the phone
jokes.punchline: 'In this case, he gave me both' — confirming that Andrew gave Selina herpes and an unwanted child (Catherine) in one devastating kicker
jokes.explanation: A structurally perfect dark joke. The simile builds ('herpes virus or an unwanted child') and then the 'in this case, he gave me both' lands with devastating precision — it retroactively reframes everything, including Catherine's entire existence as Selina's perspective. The economy of the kicker is exceptional. High quotability. This is Selina Meyer in her purest form.

jokes.text: Well, Amy Brookheimer just hung up on me. Doesn't get lower than that, does it?
jokes.setup: Amy hangs up on Selina mid-conversation
jokes.punchline: Selina frames being hung up on by Amy Brookheimer as the nadir of personal humiliation
jokes.explanation: Selina's hierarchy of humiliation — where Amy Brookheimer hanging up is worse than a public scandal — is very character-specific. The rhetorical 'Doesn't get lower than that' is a nice self-aware touch.

jokes.text: I just didn't wanna tell you 'I told you so,' but... / Because you didn't. / Okay. / Right? You just rolled your eyes like the world's bitchiest mime. Like you just did right there. / And there it is again, see?
jokes.setup: Catherine claims she warned Selina about Andrew
jokes.punchline: Selina catches Catherine's eye-roll and declares her 'the world's bitchiest mime' — then catches the second eye-roll in real time
jokes.explanation: 'World's bitchiest mime' is a great insult — specific and visual. The real-time catch of the second eye-roll ('And there it is again, see?') gives the joke a second beat that lands. Strong mother-daughter character comedy.

jokes.text: God, I'm really starting to understand this whole lesbian thing now. / Glad you're coming around. / I'm just getting drunk here on Gary's great bourbon from his inbred backwoods family. / What? There is zero inbreeding in my family. And only case of schizophrenia, but my uncle-grandpa had her sterilized, and we are all good...
jokes.setup: Selina is getting drunk after Andrew's betrayal and makes a joke about 'understanding the lesbian thing'; Gary's bourbon is mentioned
jokes.punchline: Gary's denial of inbreeding immediately proves the case — 'uncle-grandpa' and 'had her sterilized' in consecutive sentences
jokes.explanation: 'Uncle-grandpa' is the key term — the combination title is itself the proof of inbreeding while Gary denies it. The cascade ('only one case of schizophrenia, but my uncle-grandpa had her sterilized') escalates perfectly. Gary's obliviousness to how damning his own defense is makes it extraordinary. High originality for the specific family tree revelation. One of Gary's all-time great moments.

jokes.text: Now he's just going on and on for no apparent reason.
jokes.setup: Gary continues to defend his family after 'uncle-grandpa'
jokes.punchline: Selina narrates Gary's spiral as though he were a nature documentary subject
jokes.explanation: Selina's detached third-person narration of Gary's spiral is a nice comedic observation. 'No apparent reason' when the reason is extremely apparent is the irony. Quick but effective button.

jokes.text: Listen, honey, I want you to tell your whore of a father that I fired the art skank.
jokes.setup: Selina speaking to Catherine about Andrew
jokes.punchline: 'Whore of a father' and 'art skank' in close proximity — Selina using Catherine as a messaging intermediary for personal vendettas
jokes.explanation: 'Art skank' is a perfect Selina term for the portrait painter/Andrew's lover. The deployment of Catherine as a messenger for this message shows Selina's complete disregard for appropriate parenting. Well-characterised.

jokes.text: Ma'am, you are unstable and manipulative, and I worry about the genes you'll pass down to your child... but your ex is worse. / I appreciate that. Means a lot.
jokes.setup: Gary is offering Selina comfort in the Andrew crisis
jokes.punchline: Gary's version of comfort is 'you're terrible, but Andrew is worse' — and Selina accepts this as a genuine compliment
jokes.explanation: This is extraordinary character comedy. Gary's declaration ('unstable and manipulative... I worry about the genes') is a devastating assessment; the pivot ('but your ex is worse') is his idea of comfort; Selina's sincere 'I appreciate that. Means a lot.' reveals the emotional depth of their bond — she genuinely finds it comforting to be told she's merely bad rather than the worst. Near-perfect character integration for both. One of the episode's top moments.

jokes.text: You're like a son to me. / Thank you, ma'am. / You can call me 'Mom.' / Thank you, Mom. / Except, you know, it doesn't quite sound right, you know, when you hear it out loud. / So, let's just stick with the ma'am.
jokes.setup: Gary's comfort has touched Selina; she elevates their relationship
jokes.punchline: She invites Gary to call her 'Mom,' he does, she immediately retracts the offer upon hearing it aloud
jokes.explanation: The structure is beautiful: warmth extended, accepted, immediately retracted. 'Doesn't quite sound right, you know, when you hear it out loud' is Selina taking back the moment of vulnerability in real time. Gary's cheerful 'Yeah, yeah, thank you' as he accepts the demotion back to 'ma'am' is the perfect kicker. This sequence is Selina/Gary at their most nuanced and funny simultaneously. Exceptional character integration.

jokes.text: And I want all the touch screens to say that they're powered by my clean energy initiative 'cause those ecotards love that shit.
jokes.setup: Selina is planning library exhibits
jokes.punchline: 'Ecotards' — Selina's contemptuous term for environmentalists whose support she is courting through this branding exercise
jokes.explanation: 'Ecotards' is a Selina-specific coinage that reveals her contempt for the very constituency she's pandering to. The cynicism is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: She's telling the world about how she was blowing Andrew in my office chair?! / No, ma'am, it's mostly about how Andrew pursued her, how she's the victim, how she lost her job, and then there's this nickname stuff.
jokes.setup: Selina reacts to hearing Helen Wright is going public
jokes.punchline: Selina's assumption of what's being publicized ('blowing Andrew') vs. the actual coverage (Andrew as predator, Helen as victim) — Selina has the wrong read
jokes.explanation: Selina's instinct to assume the worst portrayal of Helen (rather than the worst portrayal of Andrew) reveals her default mode. The 'nickname stuff' teaser is good setup for the next beat.

jokes.text: All right, your fly is down. / What? / And both of your shoes are untied. / How did that happen? / I got one theory.
jokes.setup: Gary reports that Helen signed an NDA but they didn't pay her, so it's unenforceable.
jokes.punchline: Gary's observation that both Selina's fly is down AND both shoes are untied — followed by 'I got one theory' (implying Andrew sabotaged her clothing before the fundraiser) — is a visual callback to Andrew's treachery and a physical comedy beat.
jokes.explanation: The setup of fly + both shoes untied creates the comic image of Selina in a state of wardrobe chaos at her own fundraiser. Gary's 'I got one theory' (Andrew did it) is the callback punchline — it reframes Andrew's betrayal as extending even to petty sabotage of her outfit. A good visual gag that pays off the Andrew storyline comedically.

jokes.text: Apparently, Andrew kept referring to his penis as the 'First Chubby.' / Oof. / Oh, God.
jokes.setup: The staffer mentions 'nickname stuff' in the Helen Wright story
jokes.punchline: 'First Chubby' — Andrew's self-given presidential-themed nickname for his penis
jokes.explanation: Peak Veep. 'First Chubby' is the joke of the episode and possibly one of the show's all-timers. It's a presidential nickname ('First Lady,' 'First Family') applied to a penis, with the additional layer that Andrew is not even the president — his ex-wife is. The self-aggrandizement is breathtaking. Maximum originality, maximum economy, maximum character integration for Andrew. 'Oof' / 'Oh, God' as the reaction is perfectly calibrated understatement. This rivals 'Snip, snap' as a piece of comedic writing.

jokes.text: Well, we could had we paid her. / Who was in charge of that? / Andrew. / Okay, well, fucked again by the First Chubby.
jokes.setup: Selina's team realize they can't sue Helen Wright over the NDA because Andrew (who managed it) didn't pay her
jokes.punchline: 'Fucked again by the First Chubby' — the callback to the nickname, now deployed as a verb phrase describing her actual situation
jokes.explanation: The callback is executed perfectly. 'Fucked again by the First Chubby' works on literal and metaphorical levels simultaneously — she's being screwed over by Andrew (First Chubby as metonym for Andrew) and literally fucked (in the vulgar sense) by his nickname. The economy is extraordinary: five words that do double or triple duty. Tied with 101 as the episode's best constructed joke.

jokes.text: And both of your shoes are untied. / How did that happen? / I got one theory.
jokes.setup: Gary points out that both Selina's shoes are untied after chaotic events
jokes.punchline: 'I got one theory' — Gary implying the crisis caused by Andrew explains even the mundane disaster of untied shoes
jokes.explanation: A nice small joke — 'I got one theory' as a Gary understatement about how Andrew has destabilized everything, even shoelaces. Good economy.

jokes.text: I'm the only one who didn't do anything wrong in this 'trifuckta.' And now I'm wearing the scarlet letter? / Oh, that is your color.
jokes.setup: Selina complains about being blamed in a situation she wasn't directly responsible for
jokes.punchline: 'Trifuckta' (portmanteau of trifecta/fuck) plus Gary's deadpan response that scarlet is her color
jokes.explanation: 'Trifuckta' is a strong Selina coinage — the portmanteau works naturally. Gary's 'that is your color' takes the Scarlet Letter reference literally (a fashion comment) while also being true that red suits her. The double meaning makes Gary's response funnier.

jokes.text: I think that we are both victims... / How the fuck can you say that?! / I can say that...
jokes.setup: Selina confronts protesters outside; a protester screams about Helen Wright being the victim
jokes.punchline: Selina's claim to also be a victim infuriates the crowd — her instinct to center herself in any narrative
jokes.explanation: Selina making herself the co-victim in her ex-husband's affair is very on-brand. The protester's outrage is earned and the comedy is in how oblivious Selina is to how bad this sounds.

jokes.text: And speaking of politics, he's 28 feet tall and only bathes once a year. That's right, Jane. The Lincoln Memorial has been undergoing its annual cleaning.
jokes.setup: Danny pivots from political scandal coverage to a puff piece about the Lincoln Memorial
jokes.punchline: 'He's 28 feet tall and only bathes once a year' as a description of Lincoln before revealing it's about the monument — the transition is itself the joke
jokes.explanation: 'Only bathes once a year' is funny for the Lincoln image; the reveal that it's the statue's cleaning is a good misdirection structure. The local-news perkiness of Danny's delivery is also the joke.

jokes.text: Wrap that wire around my neck until I'm dead.
jokes.setup: The newscast wraps; Danny has just done a piece he clearly finds humiliating
jokes.punchline: Danny asks a technician to kill him with the microphone wire — professional self-loathing rendered as suicidal ideation
jokes.explanation: Danny's self-loathing is a nice character beat for someone who's been humiliating himself in local news. The specific request ('around my neck until I'm dead') is appropriately operatic. Clean, economic dark comedy.

jokes.text: I don't like the word shrill because it's so misogynistic, but in this case... / Believe me, I understand. I mean, there isn't a tampon in the world big enough to soak up that mess out there, my God.
jokes.setup: The Smith president begins to describe the protesting students diplomatically
jokes.punchline: Selina cuts her off with a tampon metaphor for crowd management — extremely crude and delivered with total naturalism
jokes.explanation: The setup ('I don't like the word shrill because it's so misogynistic') makes the Smith president seem principled, then Selina follows with a much more egregious body-based dismissal. The tampon metaphor for the crowd is original and very Selina — she weaponizes feminist vocabulary cynically while saying something deeply undignified.

jokes.text: Selina, we have to put the library on hold. / What?! / Because of those 18-year-old bitches out there?!
jokes.setup: The Smith president delivers bad news about the library
jokes.punchline: Selina's response frames feminist student protestors as 'bitches' — the speed of the mask-slip is the joke
jokes.explanation: The speed from bad news to 'bitches' is the joke. Selina's contempt for the very demographic she's been courting is perfectly revealed. Functional character comedy.

jokes.text: No, that's like Princess Di hiring Camilla Parker Bowles to be her limo driver. I mean, I'm just... you know, although in that case, it would've worked out better for her, obviously, but...
jokes.setup: Someone suggests Selina publicly rehire Helen Wright (the 'art skank') to defuse the protest
jokes.punchline: The Princess Diana/Camilla/limo driver joke — hiring her romantic rival to drive her car — then Selina realizes mid-sentence that in Diana's case, that would have prevented her death in a car crash
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's great jokes. The Diana metaphor is well-set-up; the dark pivot ('although in that case, it would've worked out better for her, obviously') is where it becomes exceptional. Selina catches herself mid-point and acknowledges the flaw in her analogy in real time — but the flaw leads somewhere much darker (Diana's death in a car crash). The self-correction is funnier than the original point. High originality for finding the specific historical problem with the chosen metaphor. One of the very best in the episode.

jokes.text: why don't you and I go out to dinner, okay, and if we... if we need more time, you know, let's go back to my hotel and we can have that Chardonnay... / I'm in a committed relationship.
jokes.setup: Selina, facing the library collapse, pivots to a personal approach with the Smith president
jokes.punchline: The Smith president shuts down what sounds like a romantic advance with 'I'm in a committed relationship' — closing the loop on the Chardonnay college night
jokes.explanation: The Chardonnay callback pays off here — Selina's negotiating tactic is essentially a romantic proposition, and the Smith president's 'committed relationship' response closes the arc of the college night implication. The cringe of Selina using seduction to save her library is maximum.

jokes.text: Yeah, I really, honestly... I have no idea what you're talking about. You're just, like...
jokes.setup: After the Smith president says 'I'm in a committed relationship'
jokes.punchline: Selina's immediate retreat to total denial and confusion — 'I have no idea what you're talking about' as a deflection of a romantic advance she absolutely made
jokes.explanation: Selina's denial is immediate and complete. The 'You're just, like...' trailing off is a great touch — she can't even finish the dismissal. Good character integration.

jokes.text: I can't believe I spent the night in jail. It's a piddly DUI. In Nevada, that's practically a resume builder. There's a guy driving drunk on the state flag right next to the guy beating his wife. / No, it's a five-pointed star.
jokes.setup: Buddy is upset about spending the night in jail for a DUI; he contextualizes it as normal in Nevada
jokes.punchline: The 'guy driving drunk and beating his wife on the state flag' is immediately corrected — 'it's a five-pointed star' — deflating the absurdist premise with dry literalism
jokes.explanation: A well-constructed two-beat: Buddy's absurdist regionalism ('DUI is a resume builder in Nevada') peaks with the flag description; Amy's flat correction ('It's a five-pointed star') is the perfect deadpan puncture. The structure is clean and the correction is funnier than the original claim.

jokes.text: Hey, hobos, get off the Internet. This isn't the library.
jokes.setup: Amy is in what seems to be a public or campaign space; she addresses people on computers
jokes.punchline: 'This isn't the library' — the line lands because Selina's quest for a library is the episode's A-plot; the casual dismissal of hobos using a public library's Internet accidentally echoes the whole episode
jokes.explanation: Gets a small thematic bonus for the library echo. Amy's contempt for 'hobos' is on-brand. Solid quick line.

jokes.text: Dashcam footage: 'Ooh, easy! Easy, pretty lady. What, are you on the rag?' ... 'Oh, you're all hot and juicy now. I bet if I take you home to bed, you're just gonna lie there with your BlackBerry and no dirty talk for Uncle Buddy then.'
jokes.setup: Buddy's dashcam DUI arrest footage is being watched by the team
jokes.punchline: The specific progression of Buddy's drunk verbal assault on the officer — from 'on the rag' to 'hot and juicy' to 'Uncle Buddy' — escalates to a very specific and damning fantasy
jokes.explanation: 'Uncle Buddy' is the gem here — the self-naming in a bizarre sexual proposition reveals a very specific kind of political-man delusion. The escalation from offensive to deeply strange is well-paced.

jokes.text: You know, I blacked out. I... I don't remember any of that. Okay? Can I count on your vote?
jokes.setup: After the devastating dashcam footage, Buddy watches it with the team
jokes.punchline: 'Can I count on your vote?' — immediately pivoting from total moral collapse to campaign mode
jokes.explanation: The pivot from 'I blacked out' to 'Can I count on your vote?' is a perfect politician joke — the total inability to process the situation in human terms, immediately reverting to electoral mode. The speed of the pivot is the key.

jokes.text: I love Sting's solo stuff. Say your favorite album on three. One, two, three... / 'Soul Cages.' / Come on, get out of here! / Stop it. / Are you serious? / What else is it gonna be?
jokes.setup: Dan is hitting on Kristal; they discover a shared interest in Sting
jokes.punchline: Both saying 'Soul Cages' simultaneously — an obscure enough Sting album that the coincidence is both charming and slightly ridiculous
jokes.explanation: 'Soul Cages' is a deliberately left-field choice — the joke is that Dan's pickup move of fake Sting enthusiasm accidentally works because she also likes Sting. 'What else is it gonna be?' sells it as genuine. Competent but not exceptional.

jokes.text: You have a rockin' body. You know, go ahead and order a dessert. I know you had an appetizer... / Oh, I'm not a dessert kind of girl.
jokes.setup: Dan is complimenting Kristal during their date
jokes.punchline: The classic 'you can eat dessert, you have the body for it' compliment lands ironically as a callback to the dessert-but-not-appetizer rule from the date setup — Kristal already had an appetizer, so dessert is 'not allowed' by the rules
jokes.explanation: The callback to the dessert rule is implicit rather than explicit — the comedy depends on the audience remembering. Competent but weak as a standalone joke.

jokes.text: This predator has roofied more women than Kappa Alpha Cosby. Okay, that's not an actual fraternity.
jokes.setup: Jonah is trying to warn Kristal about Dan
jokes.punchline: Jonah invents 'Kappa Alpha Cosby' as a fraternity name — then self-corrects, acknowledging it's not real, which itself is funny
jokes.explanation: 'Kappa Alpha Cosby' is a good joke; the self-correction ('that's not an actual fraternity') is a nice meta-touch. Jonah's attempt at sophisticated wit is undermined by his need to explain himself.

jokes.text: So, unless you enjoy the thrill of waking up in a basement torture dungeon... / No. / I'd say call it a night. / You know, maybe I should go. / Kristal, it's fine. It's just an unfinished basement, and he's just fucking with you.
jokes.setup: Jonah escalates his warnings about Dan to 'basement torture dungeon'
jokes.punchline: Dan's correction — 'it's just an unfinished basement' — confirms he has one, which doesn't help his case
jokes.explanation: 'It's just an unfinished basement' as a rebuttal that confirms Dan does have a basement is a great structural inversion — the denial admits the fact while disputing the characterization. Strong construction.

jokes.text: This is for trapping me in a job that makes me long for the days of Selina Meyer. And I'm gonna fuck your girlfriend.
jokes.setup: Dan turns on Jonah after losing Kristal; he delivers his retribution speech
jokes.punchline: 'A job that makes me long for the days of Selina Meyer' — working for Jonah is apparently worse than working for Selina; then the petty threat
jokes.explanation: The Selina Meyer reference as the calibration point for 'bad' — working for Jonah is worse than working for Selina — is a great inter-plot connection. Dan's petty follow-through ('I'm gonna fuck your girlfriend') is very Dan.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, could you move your head a little bit more to the light, ma'am? / Yeah, I'm not gonna do that.
jokes.setup: The portrait sitting resumes; the painter makes a request
jokes.punchline: Selina's flat refusal — 'I'm not gonna do that' — with no explanation or negotiation; she has reached her limit
jokes.explanation: Selina at the end of her rope — total refusal with no explanation. The economy is perfect; the comedy is in knowing everything that's happened today to bring her to this point.

jokes.text: Smith is a no. / Well, you can pack up your crayons and get the fuck out of here.
jokes.setup: Smith College has also rejected Selina's library application
jokes.punchline: Selina dismisses the portrait painter with 'pack up your crayons' — misdirecting her rage at the nearest target, calling oil paints 'crayons'
jokes.explanation: 'Pack up your crayons' is a nice contemptuous diminishment of the painter's art (reducing oil painting to children's art supplies). The misdirection of rage is classic Selina — she can't fire the library, so she fires the painter.

jokes.text: If there's one thing I learned in the Air National Guard, it is respect for a woman in uniform. I have no hate in my heart. I love women, especially my beautiful fiancée, Amy.
jokes.setup: Buddy gives a press conference to address his DUI dashcam scandal
jokes.punchline: Citing Air National Guard lessons about respecting women seconds after dashcam footage of him sexually harassing a female officer; claiming love for women while 'beautiful fiancée Amy' is a detail — which Amy?
jokes.explanation: The irony of the Air National Guard respect-for-women speech given the dashcam footage is the core joke. 'Beautiful fiancée, Amy' lands differently knowing Amy Brookheimer is involved in his campaign — is she the fiancée? Functional political satire.

jokes.text: Heavenly Father, warden of eternal light, teach us to love women, but in a way that is respectful. And also, Your compassion. And amen.
jokes.setup: Buddy's press conference ends with a prayer
jokes.punchline: The prayer asks God to teach love of women 'but in a way that is respectful' — as if respectful love of women is a specific skill requiring divine intervention; 'And also, Your compassion' is thrown in as an afterthought
jokes.explanation: 'Teach us to love women, but in a way that is respectful' is a brilliant final joke. The 'but in a way that is respectful' qualifier suggests that by default, their love of women is disrespectful and they need divine help to correct it. The sincerity of the delivery and the profound self-incrimination make it land hard. 'And also, Your compassion' as a lazy add-on is the perfect capper. A great ending to the episode.

tmdb_overview: Selina attends the opening of President Hughes' library. Jonah gets advice on how to rise in the political ranks; Dan suffers through Jane's whims; Amy releases a negative attack ad.
insight: Political chaos sustains 72 jokes across 44 minutes despite zero standout punchlines.
